macro_segments.title: Opening remarks and the structure of the first Mishnah on two which is four cases

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Shevuot 3 and summary of the lengthy opening Mishnah
The focus on impurity of the Temple and its atonement in the Mishnah
Connection between Yom Kippur atonement and the scapegoat ritual
Why the opening Mishnah devotes focus to Temple impurity rather than oaths
Broader context from Sanhedrin and Makkot on transgressions and punishment
Conceptual analogy between false oaths and Temple impurity
The role of the Masechet in understanding how oaths invoke God's presence
Student questions on Yom Kippur atonement and sins between people

macro_segments.title: Gemara's question on why Shevuot follows Makkot and why the Mishnah lists all two-which-is-four cases together

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's opening question: Michti—why does Shevuot come after Makkot
The ordering of tractates based on size and the connection to Sanhedrin
Why the full list of two-which-is-four is presented in Shevuot
The Torah's juxtaposition of oaths and Temple impurity cases
Explanation that oaths and tumah appear together in the same Torah passage
Reasoning for presenting all cases together despite belonging to different tractates
Rabbi's editorial process in compiling the Mishnah and collecting similar structures

macro_segments.title: Why the Mishnah opens with Shevuot and then devotes two chapters to Temple impurity

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's answer: since the impurity material is relatively short, it is addressed first
Metaphor of cutting off and untying—completing the impurity discussion quickly
Plan to focus on oaths after completing the impurity material

macro_segments.title: Digression on the laws of beard and sidelocks from Makkot, as reflected in Tosafot

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's ruling on using a straight razor versus other tools on sidelocks
Distinction between what is prohibited on the beard versus sidelocks
Rambam's disagreement on using scissors-like tools for sidelocks
Practical application: why contemporary shavers cannot be used on sidelocks
Tosafot's second ruling: both giver and receiver of a shave transgress
The case of a non-Jewish barber and obligations in commandments

macro_segments.title: The list of two-which-is-four: carrying on Shabbat, leprosy spots, and oaths

micro_segments.title: Carrying on Shabbat: the two cases of poor person and householder
The four ways of carrying between private and public domains
Leprosy spots: two types mentioned in Torah (se'et and baheret) and their subcategories
Question: who is the author of our Mishnah—not Rabbi Ishmael nor Rabbi Akiva

macro_segments.title: Dispute between Rabbi Ishmael and Rabbi Akiva on which cases require a korban

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Ishmael's position: korban only for future-oriented oaths, not past-oriented
Rabbi Akiva's position: korban for both past and future oaths, but not all tumah cases
Problem: neither rabbi holds the full two-which-is-four for both categories
Gemara's answer: the Mishnah can accommodate both views with partial applications

macro_segments.title: Resolution through a different interpretation: the Mishnah speaks of lashes, not korban

micro_segments.title: Alternative interpretation: the Mishnah's two-which-is-four refers to lashes, not korban
Past-oriented oaths clearly bring lashes via the vain oath comparison

macro_segments.title: Resolution through a different interpretation: the Mishnah speaks of lashes, not korban (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's statement that Rava explicitly compares false oaths to vain oaths
Why past-oriented oaths parallel vain oaths: violation occurs at utterance

macro_segments.title: Analysis of the four cases of false oaths and when lashes apply

micro_segments.title: Case 1: oath about the past that you ate (false)—lashes apply
Case 2: oath about the past that you did not eat (false)—lashes apply
Case 3: oath about the future that you will eat tomorrow, then you do eat—lashes apply
Case 4: oath that you won't eat, but you don't eat—passive violation, lashes unclear
The problem: how do you get lashes for a purely passive transgression

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's position on lashes for passive transgressions and the apparent contradiction

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan states the halacha follows an unattributed Mishnah (stam mishnah)
Problem: our opening Mishnah suggests you get lashes even for passive violation
But Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish agree you don't get lashes for not eating eggs

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's position on lashes for passive transgressions and the apparent contradiction (continued)

micro_segments.title: The two reasons given: either it's passive or the warning is doubtful

macro_segments.title: The Gemara finds support for Rabbi Yochanan in another Mishnah about leftover korban meat

micro_segments.title: A Mishnah states one does not get lashes for leaving over korban meat
Reason: leaving over meat is a passive transgression without an act
Alternative reason from Rabbi Yehuda: it is connected to a positive command
Rabbi Yaakov's reason: any passive transgression does not receive lashes

macro_segments.title: The Gemara questions whether Rabbi Yochanan can rely on another Mishnah to override the first

micro_segments.title: Problem: it is unclear whether the notar Mishnah supports the passive-transgression principle
The Gemara finds a better source: a Mishnah about taking two oaths not to eat
This Mishnah teaches that you only get lashes if you actively violate the oath

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Rabbi compiled contradictory teachings; the later ruling is that passive violations do not incur lashes

micro_segments.title: How can Rabbi himself have taught two contradictory Mishnayot about passive violations
Answer: initially Rabbi taught that passive violations do incur lashes
Later, Rabbi changed his mind: passive violations do not incur lashes
But Rabbi could not revoke the earlier Mishnah as it had spread widely
So Rabbi taught a later Mishnah to clarify the correct ruling
Final summary: the two-which-is-four in the opening refers to lashes and is partially overridden by later teaching

topical_tags.term: Two which is four (Shtayim Shehein Arba)
Oath about future action (Leha'id Lo Yired)
